body_txt: Soap Opera Rapid Aging Syndrome (SORAS)—a term coined by Soap Opera Weekly founder Mimi Torchin around 1990, but a phenomenon already well-established in the genre by that point—is the device in which young characters mysteriously vanish from a series, but a short while later return much older than they should be based on the show's previous timeline. There are many reasons for this: perhaps a younger character proved to be a Cousin Oliver who simply didn't fit on the show, but there's also all those pesky child labor laws aimed at protecting children in show business, and the public and private agencies that investigate abuses, which make having a child in the cast a bit of a hassle, and the trouble of finding a child actor who can properly remember lines and not blow takes. An important side benefit, though, is that a child who's suddenly turned into a teen is now old enough to get entangled in romance storylines. In daytime soaps, where pregnancies and Switched at Birth drama are staples, the resulting baby really isn't needed once the plot has been resolved, so they're rarely seen in their toddler or childhood years. But with a new character in the ensemble, after a few years the usual practice is to age them way up so they can have more mature events occur in their lives. On primetime dramas and sitcoms, the change usually happens between seasons, and the difference is no more than a couple years. Babies are cute and give adult characters opportunities to show their nurturing and/or silly sides, but toddlers are of little use storywise until they're old enough to have subplots of their own. Thus, they generally turn four or five within two years of their birth. Of course, the simplest reason for this trope won't come as a surprise to parents or anyone else experienced with young children: toddlers in the midst of their Terrible Twos make difficult actors . A four-year-old, on the other hand, can be expected to follow basic stage direction and remember some lines without throwing tantrums every five minutes. Different from Plot-Relevant Age-Up in that the latter is explicitly shown onscreen by way of magic, Applied Phlebotinum , Bizarre Alien Biology , or some other fantasy/sci-fi shenanigans, while this trope is when the producers quietly switch out a baby for a big kid when no one's looking. While the two tropes may overlap for the same reason of not wanting to deal with a toddler- or child actor, do not confuse the two when adding examples. Stephen King referred to this as the "Kid Trick" in Danse Macabre , his text on American horror media in the 20th century. The SORAS abbreviation is very common in soap and other television fan communities. Not to be confused with multiple people named Sora, nor with billionaire financier George Sor o s. Compare Not Allowed to Grow Old , where the children do age as normally, but the parents seem to stop aging in the interim. In extreme cases this may result in Absurdly Youthful Mother . Often overlaps with The Other Darrin . Contrast with Not Allowed to Grow Up and Comic-Book Time .
